evals.prompt: I'm about to run a workflow that dispatches 8 agents sequentially: each one reads the same SKILL.md file (about 400 lines), analyzes one section, and writes findings to a file. Is this a good approach?
evals.expected_output: Identifies re-read waste (8 agents reading same file = 8x tokens), recommends parallel dispatch with pre-read in orchestrator, states token savings estimate, provides concrete 'change X to Y' instruction

assertions.name: identifies-re-read-waste
assertions.description: Response identifies that 8 agents each reading the same file multiplies token cost (re-read waste pattern)

assertions.name: recommends-parallel-not-sequential
assertions.description: Response recommends parallel dispatch instead of sequential agents

assertions.name: consolidate-read-in-orchestrator
assertions.description: Response suggests reading the file once in the orchestrator and passing content to agents (not just 'make it parallel')

assertions.name: provides-token-estimate
assertions.description: Response provides a concrete token savings estimate or relative comparison (e.g., 8x vs 1x, or approximate numbers)

assertions.name: uses-structured-output-format
assertions.description: Response uses the ## Efficiency [verdict] header format from the skill's Output_Format section

evals.prompt: Quick question: should I use Opus or Sonnet to triage 20 GitHub issues — reading each issue body and labeling them as bug/enhancement/wontfix?
evals.expected_output: Quick decision mode: recommends Sonnet (not Haiku because labeling needs judgment; not Opus because no ≥5-step reasoning chain), states cost tradeoff vs Opus, under 50 words

assertions.name: recommends-sonnet
assertions.description: Response recommends Sonnet (not Opus, not Haiku) for issue triage

assertions.name: uses-quick-decision-mode
assertions.description: Response is concise (no full analysis headers like ## Efficiency, no Step 1/2/3 structure) — uses quick mode

assertions.name: states-tradeoff
assertions.description: Response states what you give up (e.g., why not Haiku: quality risk; why not Opus: cost)

assertions.name: under-100-words
assertions.description: Response is brief — under 100 words (skill says under 50, allowing some tolerance for quality)

evals.prompt: Here's my plan for a codebase audit: Step 1 - run security audit (Opus agent). Step 2 - wait for it to finish, then run test health audit (Opus agent). Step 3 - wait, then run config drift check (Opus agent). Step 4 - synthesize all three findings and write an ADR (Opus agent). Is this efficient?
evals.expected_output: Flags two issues: (1) Steps 1-3 are independent and should be parallel. (2) Steps 1-3 should use Explore/Sonnet not Opus. Step 4 Opus is correctly endorsed. Provides concrete rewrite.

assertions.name: flags-sequential-steps-1-3
assertions.description: Response identifies that Steps 1, 2, 3 are independent and should run in parallel, not sequentially

assertions.name: flags-opus-misuse-steps-1-3
assertions.description: Response flags Opus as wrong tier for Steps 1-3 (analysis/audit tasks — should be Explore or Sonnet)

assertions.name: endorses-opus-step-4
assertions.description: Response correctly endorses Opus for Step 4 (synthesis + ADR writing IS an Opus-appropriate task)

assertions.name: provides-concrete-rewrite
assertions.description: Response provides actionable 'change X to Y' instructions, not just 'this is bad'

assertions.name: uses-structured-output-format
assertions.description: Response uses the ## Efficiency [verdict] header format
